summary: In this very brief post, Spolsky makes a humorous observation about Barbra Streisand dropping the 'a' from her first name in 1960, nearly 40 years before the internet existed. He jokes that the unusual spelling makes her much easier to find in search engines and on Amazon. He sarcastically credits her with 'incredible foresight' for inadvertently optimizing her name for digital searchability decades before it would matter.
key_points: Unique name spellings provide a significant advantage in digital search and discovery
Decisions made long before a technology exists can accidentally become perfectly suited to it
The internet rewards distinctiveness and uniqueness in ways no one could have predicted
money_quotes: In 1960, almost 40 years before the Internet came along, Barbra Streisand drops the "a" from her first name.
Of course, with the unusual spelling, it's much easier to find her in search engines, on Amazon, etc.
key_insight: Unique, distinctive naming — even if accidental — turns out to be a powerful advantage in the age of search engines and digital discovery.
